ending: Small Gods ends with the tyranny of Omnia's Church broken and a new, gentler order begun. At the point of Brutha's public execution, the Great God Om finally reclaims his strength: carried up by an eagle and dropped onto Deacon Vorbis, he kills the man who had seized control of the Church, and as the crowd sees Brutha's courage, true belief floods back to Om. Brutha survives and becomes the new prophet. Instead of wielding the power he inherits as his predecessors did, he strikes a new bargain with his god and remakes Omnia, abolishing the Quisition and steering the nation toward tolerance, literacy, and a lasting peace, while stopping the war Vorbis had provoked. The story then jumps a hundred years forward to Brutha's own death as an old man. When Death comes for him and he must cross the desert that lies beyond life, Brutha finds the spirit of Vorbis still standing there, alone and terrified, unmoved since his death because he had never truly cared for anyone but himself. Brutha takes Vorbis's hand and walks with him across the desert, choosing kindness toward his old enemy as his final act.
in_short: In the theocratic nation of Omnia, the Great God Om finds his power gone: because true belief, not mere Church membership, feeds a god, and only the kind, perfectly remembering novice Brutha genuinely believes in him, Om has shrunk to a nearly helpless talking tortoise. Brutha, the only one who can hear him, is taken into the service of Deacon Vorbis, a cold master of the Church's torturing Quisition, and travels with him to the philosopher-city of Ephebe, where forbidden ideas, including that the world is a disc on a turtle, begin to shake Brutha's unquestioning faith. Vorbis engineers treachery and war; after a shipwreck Brutha carries the injured Vorbis across the desert out of sheer decency. In Omnia, Vorbis makes himself prophet and condemns Brutha to death, but Om intervenes, dropped by an eagle onto Vorbis to kill him as belief and his power return. Brutha becomes prophet, ends the Quisition, and turns Omnia toward tolerance and peace. A century later, dying, he helps even Vorbis's frightened soul cross the desert of death.

recaps.text: In the theocratic nation of Omnia, whose powerful Church enforces worship of the Great God Om, a slow but kind novice named Brutha, blessed with a perfect memory, is astonished when a small one-eyed tortoise begins speaking to him and claims to be Om himself. Om has discovered a terrible truth: a god's power comes from genuine belief, and though millions attend his Church out of fear and habit, Brutha is the only person left who truly believes in him, which is why he has dwindled to a near-helpless tortoise able to speak to no one else. Brutha's gift for remembering everything draws the notice of Deacon Vorbis, a cold and formidable figure of the Quisition, the Church's machinery of inquisition and torture. Vorbis takes Brutha into his service and includes him in a diplomatic mission to the distant city of Ephebe.

The mission reaches Ephebe, a coastal city of philosophers and argument, utterly unlike rigid Omnia. There Brutha, carrying Om the tortoise with him, encounters thinkers such as the blind, barrel-dwelling philosopher Didactylos and his inventor nephew Urn. He is confronted by ideas forbidden at home, above all the claim that the world is a disc riding on a great turtle, which contradicts the Church's teaching that it is a sphere. Brutha, who has never questioned anything, begins to be troubled by what he learns and by the gap between his Church's cruelty and the god he actually hears. Vorbis, meanwhile, pursues his own designs behind the mission's official purpose.

Vorbis's true intentions surface as he engineers treachery to serve the Church's ambitions, turning the diplomatic errand toward conflict between Omnia and Ephebe. In the upheaval Brutha, Vorbis, and Om flee Ephebe by sea, but their vessel is wrecked and they are cast into the desert. There Brutha, out of simple decency, carries the injured and helpless Vorbis across the burning wasteland on his back, keeping alive the man who embodies everything cruel about his Church. Om, still a vulnerable tortoise dependent on Brutha, is carried along, and Brutha's steady kindness under extremity marks him as something the Church never made him.

Back in Omnia, Vorbis consolidates his power and has himself installed as the Church's new prophet and supreme head, staging events to confirm his authority. Brutha, whose growing conscience and knowledge make him dangerous, is condemned as a heretic and prepared for public execution, to be tortured to death on an iron instrument before the assembled faithful. Om, still nearly powerless, is desperate to save his one believer but can do almost nothing in his tortoise form. The confrontation between Brutha's plain goodness and Vorbis's ruthless certainty comes to a head before the crowds of the Citadel.

At the climax Om finally acts: he arranges to be carried aloft by an eagle and dropped from a great height onto Vorbis, killing him, and as the crowd witnesses Brutha's courage and the god's returning power, real belief surges back and Om is restored to strength. Brutha survives and becomes the new prophet of Omnia. Rather than continue the Church's tyranny, he negotiates a new understanding with Om, ending the Quisition and turning Omnia toward tolerance, learning, and peace, and he halts the war that Vorbis's schemes had set in motion. The book closes many years later: Brutha dies as an old man, and Death comes for him. In the desert that souls must cross, Brutha finds the terrified spirit of Vorbis, who has stood alone and frightened there ever since his death, and takes him by the hand to help him make the journey, returning kindness for cruelty one last time.
